1→# YAML support for the Go language 2→ 3→[![PkgGoDev](https://pkg.go.dev/badge/github.com/goccy/go-yaml)](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/goccy/go-yaml) 4→![Go](https://github.com/goccy/go-yaml/workflows/Go/badge.svg) 5→[![codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/goccy/go-yaml/branch/master/graph/badge.svg)](https://codecov.io/gh/goccy/go-yaml) 6→[![Go Report Card](https://goreportcard.com/badge/github.com/goccy/go-yaml)](https://goreportcard.com/report/github.com/goccy/go-yaml) 7→ 8→ 9→ 10→# Why a new library? 11→ 12→As of this writing, there already exists a de facto standard library for YAML processing for Go: [https://github.com/go-yaml/yaml](https://github.com/go-yaml/yaml). However we feel that some features are lacking, namely: 13→ 14→- Pretty format for error notifications 15→- Direct manipulation of YAML abstract syntax tree 16→- Support for `Anchor` and `Alias` when marshaling 17→- Allow referencing elements declared in another file via anchors 18→ 19→# Features 20→ 21→- Pretty format for error notifications 22→- Supports `Scanner` or `Lexer` or `Parser` as public API 23→- Supports `Anchor` and `Alias` to Marshaler 24→- Allow referencing elements declared in another file via anchors 25→- Extract value or AST by YAMLPath ( YAMLPath is like a JSONPath ) 26→ 27→# Installation 28→ 29→```sh 30→go get -u github.com/goccy/go-yaml 31→``` 32→ 33→# Synopsis 34→ 35→## 1. Simple Encode/Decode 36→ 37→Has an interface like `go-yaml/yaml` using `reflect` 38→ 39→```go 40→var v struct { 41→ A int 42→ B string 43→} 44→v.A = 1 45→v.B = "hello" 46→bytes, err := yaml.Marshal(v) 47→if err != nil { 48→ //... 49→} 50→fmt.Println(string(bytes)) // "a: 1\nb: hello\n" 51→``` 52→ 53→```go 54→ yml := ` 55→%YAML 1.2 56→--- 57→a: 1 58→b: c 59→` 60→var v struct { 61→ A int 62→ B string 63→} 64→if err := yaml.Unmarshal([]byte(yml), &v); err != nil { 65→ //... 66→} 67→``` 68→ 69→To control marshal/unmarshal behavior, you can use the `yaml` tag. 70→ 71→```go 72→ yml := `--- 73→foo: 1 74→bar: c 75→` 76→var v struct { 77→ A int `yaml:"foo"` 78→ B string `yaml:"bar"` 79→} 80→if err := yaml.Unmarshal([]byte(yml), &v); err != nil { 81→ //... 82→} 83→``` 84→ 85→For convenience, we also accept the `json` tag. Note that not all options from 86→the `json` tag will have significance when parsing YAML documents. If both 87→tags exist, `yaml` tag will take precedence. 88→ 89→```go 90→ yml := `--- 91→foo: 1 92→bar: c 93→` 94→var v struct { 95→ A int `json:"foo"` 96→ B string `json:"bar"` 97→} 98→if err := yaml.Unmarshal([]byte(yml), &v); err != nil { 99→ //... 100→} 101→``` 102→ 103→For custom marshal/unmarshaling, implement either `Bytes` or `Interface` variant of marshaler/unmarshaler. The difference is that while `BytesMarshaler`/`BytesUnmarshaler` behaves like [`encoding/json`](https://pkg.go.dev/encoding/json) and `InterfaceMarshaler`/`InterfaceUnmarshaler` behaves like [`gopkg.in/yaml.v2`](https://pkg.go.dev/gopkg.in/yaml.v2). 104→ 105→Semantically both are the same, but they differ in performance. Because indentation matters in YAML, you cannot simply accept a valid YAML fragment from a Marshaler, and expect it to work when it is attached to the parent container's serialized form. Therefore when we receive use the `BytesMarshaler`, which returns `[]byte`, we must decode it once to figure out how to make it work in the given context. If you use the `InterfaceMarshaler`, we can skip the decoding. 106→ 107→If you are repeatedly marshaling complex objects, the latter is always better 108→performance wise. But if you are, for example, just providing a choice between 109→a config file format that is read only once, the former is probably easier to 110→code. 111→ 112→## 2. Reference elements declared in another file 113→ 114→`testdata` directory contains `anchor.yml` file: 115→ 116→```shell 117→├── testdata 118→   └── anchor.yml 119→``` 120→ 121→And `anchor.yml` is defined as follows: 122→ 123→```yaml 124→a: &a 125→ b: 1 126→ c: hello 127→``` 128→ 129→Then, if `yaml.ReferenceDirs("testdata")` option is passed to `yaml.Decoder`, 130→ `Decoder` tries to find the anchor definition from YAML files the under `testdata` directory. 131→ 132→```go 133→buf := bytes.NewBufferString("a: *a\n") 134→dec := yaml.NewDecoder(buf, yaml.ReferenceDirs("testdata")) 135→var v struct { 136→ A struct { 137→ B int 138→ C string 139→ } 140→} 141→if err := dec.Decode(&v); err != nil { 142→ //... 143→} 144→fmt.Printf("%+v\n", v) // {A:{B:1 C:hello}} 145→``` 146→ 147→## 3. Encode with `Anchor` and `Alias` 148→ 149→### 3.1. Explicitly declared `Anchor` name and `Alias` name 150→ 151→If you want to use `anchor` or `alias`, you can define it as a struct tag. 152→ 153→```go 154→type T struct { 155→ A int 156→ B string 157→} 158→var v struct { 159→ C *T `yaml:"c,anchor=x"` 160→ D *T `yaml:"d,alias=x"` 161→} 162→v.C = &T{A: 1, B: "hello"} 163→v.D = v.C 164→bytes, err := yaml.Marshal(v) 165→if err != nil { 166→ panic(err) 167→} 168→fmt.Println(string(bytes)) 169→/* 170→c: &x 171→ a: 1 172→ b: hello 173→d: *x 174→*/ 175→``` 176→ 177→### 3.2. Implicitly declared `Anchor` and `Alias` names 178→ 179→If you do not explicitly declare the anchor name, the default behavior is to 180→use the equivalent of `strings.ToLower($FieldName)` as the name of the anchor. 181→ 182→If you do not explicitly declare the alias name AND the value is a pointer 183→to another element, we look up the anchor name by finding out which anchor 184→field the value is assigned to by looking up its pointer address. 185→ 186→```go 187→type T struct { 188→ I int 189→ S string 190→} 191→var v struct { 192→ A *T `yaml:"a,anchor"` 193→ B *T `yaml:"b,anchor"` 194→ C *T `yaml:"c,alias"` 195→ D *T `yaml:"d,alias"` 196→} 197→v.A = &T{I: 1, S: "hello"} 198→v.B = &T{I: 2, S: "world"} 199→v.C = v.A // C has same pointer address to A 200→v.D = v.B // D has same pointer address to B 201→bytes, err := yaml.Marshal(v) 202→if err != nil { 203→ //... 204→} 205→fmt.Println(string(bytes)) 206→/* 207→a: &a 208→ i: 1 209→ s: hello 210→b: &b 211→ i: 2 212→ s: world 213→c: *a 214→d: *b 215→*/ 216→``` 217→ 218→### 3.3 MergeKey and Alias 219→ 220→Merge key and alias ( `<<: *alias` ) can be used by embedding a structure with the `inline,alias` tag. 221→ 222→```go 223→type Person struct { 224→ *Person `yaml:",omitempty,inline,alias"` // embed Person type for default value 225→ Name string `yaml:",omitempty"` 226→ Age int `yaml:",omitempty"` 227→} 228→defaultPerson := &Person{ 229→ Name: "John Smith", 230→ Age: 20, 231→} 232→people := []*Person{ 233→ { 234→ Person: defaultPerson, // assign default value 235→ Name: "Ken", // override Name property 236→ Age: 10, // override Age property 237→ }, 238→ { 239→ Person: defaultPerson, // assign default value only 240→ }, 241→} 242→var doc struct { 243→ Default *Person `yaml:"default,anchor"` 244→ People []*Person `yaml:"people"` 245→} 246→doc.Default = defaultPerson 247→doc.People = people 248→bytes, err := yaml.Marshal(doc) 249→if err != nil { 250→ //... 251→} 252→fmt.Println(string(bytes)) 253→/* 254→default: &default 255→ name: John Smith 256→ age: 20 257→people: 258→- <<: *default 259→ name: Ken 260→ age: 10 261→- <<: *default 262→*/ 263→``` 264→ 265→## 4. Pretty Formatted Errors 266→ 267→Error values produced during parsing have two extra features over regular 268→error values. 269→ 270→First, by default, they contain extra information on the location of the error 271→from the source YAML document, to make it easier to find the error location. 272→ 273→Second, the error messages can optionally be colorized. 274→ 275→If you would like to control exactly how the output looks like, consider 276→using `yaml.FormatError`, which accepts two boolean values to 277→control turning these features on or off. 278→ 279→ 280→ 281→## 5. Use YAMLPath 282→ 283→```go 284→yml := ` 285→store: 286→ book: 287→ - author: john 288→ price: 10 289→ - author: ken 290→ price: 12 291→ bicycle: 292→ color: red 293→ price: 19.95 294→` 295→path, err := yaml.PathString("$.store.book[*].author") 296→if err != nil { 297→ //... 298→} 299→var authors []string 300→if err := path.Read(strings.NewReader(yml), &authors); err != nil { 301→ //... 302→} 303→fmt.Println(authors) 304→// [john ken] 305→``` 306→ 307→### 5.1 Print customized error with YAML source code 308→ 309→```go 310→package main 311→ 312→import ( 313→ "fmt" 314→ 315→ "github.com/goccy/go-yaml" 316→) 317→ 318→func main() { 319→ yml := ` 320→a: 1 321→b: "hello" 322→` 323→ var v struct { 324→ A int 325→ B string 326→ } 327→ if err := yaml.Unmarshal([]byte(yml), &v); err != nil { 328→ panic(err) 329→ } 330→ if v.A != 2 { 331→ // output error with YAML source 332→ path, err := yaml.PathString("$.a") 333→ if err != nil { 334→ panic(err) 335→ } 336→ source, err := path.AnnotateSource([]byte(yml), true) 337→ if err != nil { 338→ panic(err) 339→ } 340→ fmt.Printf("a value expected 2 but actual %d:\n%s\n", v.A, string(source)) 341→ } 342→} 343→``` 344→ 345→output result is the following: 346→ 347→ 348→ 349→ 350→# Tools 351→ 352→## ycat 353→ 354→print yaml file with color 355→ 356→ 357→ 358→### Installation 359→ 360→```sh 361→go install github.com/goccy/go-yaml/cmd/ycat@latest 362→``` 363→ 364→# Looking for Sponsors 365→ 366→I'm looking for sponsors this library. This library is being developed as a personal project in my spare time. If you want a quick response or problem resolution when using this library in your project, please register as a [sponsor](https://github.com/sponsors/goccy). I will cooperate as much as possible.     15→// BytesMarshaler interface may be implemented by types to customize their
    16→// behavior when being marshaled into a YAML document. The returned value
    17→// is marshaled in place of the original value implementing Marshaler.
    18→//
    19→// If an error is returned by MarshalYAML, the marshaling procedure stops
    20→// and returns with the provided error.
    21→type BytesMarshaler interface {
    22→	MarshalYAML() ([]byte, error)
    23→}
    24→
    25→// BytesMarshalerContext interface use BytesMarshaler with context.Context.
    26→type BytesMarshalerContext interface {
    27→	MarshalYAML(context.Context) ([]byte, error)
    28→}
    29→
    30→// InterfaceMarshaler interface has MarshalYAML compatible with github.com/go-yaml/yaml package.
    31→type InterfaceMarshaler interface {
    32→	MarshalYAML() (interface{}, error)
    33→}
    34→
    35→// InterfaceMarshalerContext interface use InterfaceMarshaler with context.Context.
    36→type InterfaceMarshalerContext interface {
    37→	MarshalYAML(context.Context) (interface{}, error)
    38→}
    39→
    40→// BytesUnmarshaler interface may be implemented by types to customize their
    41→// behavior when being unmarshaled from a YAML document.
    42→type BytesUnmarshaler interface {
    43→	UnmarshalYAML([]byte) error
    44→}
    45→
    46→// BytesUnmarshalerContext interface use BytesUnmarshaler with context.Context.
    47→type BytesUnmarshalerContext interface {
    48→	UnmarshalYAML(context.Context, []byte) error
    49→}
    50→
    51→// InterfaceUnmarshaler interface has UnmarshalYAML compatible with github.com/go-yaml/yaml package.
    52→type InterfaceUnmarshaler interface {
    53→	UnmarshalYAML(func(interface{}) error) error
    54→}
    55→
    56→// InterfaceUnmarshalerContext interface use InterfaceUnmarshaler with context.Context.
    57→type InterfaceUnmarshalerContext interface {
    58→	UnmarshalYAML(context.Context, func(interface{}) error) error
    59→}
    60→
    61→// MapItem is an item in a MapSlice.
    62→type MapItem struct {
    63→	Key, Value interface{}
    64→}
    65→
    66→// MapSlice encodes and decodes as a YAML map.
    67→// The order of keys is preserved when encoding and decoding.
    68→type MapSlice []MapItem
    69→
    70→// ToMap convert to map[interface{}]interface{}.
    71→func (s MapSlice) ToMap() map[interface{}]interface{} {
    72→	v := map[interface{}]interface{}{}
    73→	for _, item := range s {
    74→		v[item.Key] = item.Value
    75→	}
    76→	return v
    77→}
    78→
    79→// Marshal serializes the value provided into a YAML document. The structure
    80→// of the generated document will reflect the structure of the value itself.
    81→// Maps and pointers (to struct, string, int, etc) are accepted as the in value.
    82→//
    83→// Struct fields are only marshalled if they are exported (have an upper case
    84→// first letter), and are marshalled using the field name lowercased as the
    85→// default key. Custom keys may be defined via the "yaml" name in the field
    86→// tag: the content preceding the first comma is used as the key, and the
    87→// following comma-separated options are used to tweak the marshalling process.
    88→// Conflicting names result in a runtime error.
    89→//
    90→// The field tag format accepted is:
    91→//
    92→//	`(...) yaml:"[<key>][,<flag1>[,<flag2>]]" (...)`
    93→//
    94→// The following flags are currently supported:
    95→//
    96→//	omitempty    Only include the field if it's not set to the zero
    97→//	             value for the type or to empty slices or maps.
    98→//	             Zero valued structs will be omitted if all their public
    99→//	             fields are zero, unless they implement an IsZero
   100→//	             method (see the IsZeroer interface type), in which
   101→//	             case the field will be included if that method returns true.
   102→//
   103→//	flow         Marshal using a flow style (useful for structs,
   104→//	             sequences and maps).
   105→//
   106→//	inline       Inline the field, which must be a struct or a map,
   107→//	             causing all of its fields or keys to be processed as if
   108→//	             they were part of the outer struct. For maps, keys must
   109→//	             not conflict with the yaml keys of other struct fields.
   110→//
   111→//	anchor       Marshal with anchor. If want to define anchor name explicitly, use anchor=name style.
   112→//	             Otherwise, if used 'anchor' name only, used the field name lowercased as the anchor name
   113→//
   114→//	alias        Marshal with alias. If want to define alias name explicitly, use alias=name style.
   115→//	             Otherwise, If omitted alias name and the field type is pointer type,
   116→//	             assigned anchor name automatically from same pointer address.
   117→//
   118→// In addition, if the key is "-", the field is ignored.
   119→//
   120→// For example:
   121→//
   122→//	type T struct {
   123→//	    F int `yaml:"a,omitempty"`
   124→//	    B int
   125→//	}
   126→//	yaml.Marshal(&T{B: 2}) // Returns "b: 2\n"
   127→//	yaml.Marshal(&T{F: 1}) // Returns "a: 1\nb: 0\n"
   128→func Marshal(v interface{}) ([]byte, error) {
   129→	return MarshalWithOptions(v)
   130→}
   131→
   132→// MarshalWithOptions serializes the value provided into a YAML document with EncodeOptions.
   133→func MarshalWithOptions(v interface{}, opts ...EncodeOption) ([]byte, error) {
   134→	return MarshalContext(context.Background(), v, opts...)
   135→}
   136→
   137→// MarshalContext serializes the value provided into a YAML document with context.Context and EncodeOptions.
   138→func MarshalContext(ctx context.Context, v interface{}, opts ...EncodeOption) ([]byte, error) {
   139→	var buf bytes.Buffer
   140→	if err := NewEncoder(&buf, opts...).EncodeContext(ctx, v); err != nil {
   141→		return nil, errors.Wrapf(err, "failed to marshal")
   142→	}
   143→	return buf.Bytes(), nil
   144→}

All tests pass. Here's a summary of the changes:

**Root cause:** In `createDecodedNewValue` (decode.go:992), when the YAML node is null, the function immediately returned `reflect.Zero(typ)` — ignoring the `defaultVal` parameter entirely. This meant pre-initialized struct fields were zeroed out whenever their YAML key was present but null (e.g., a key with only comments as its value).

**Fix (decode.go):** Before returning the zero value, check if the type is a non-pointer struct and `defaultVal` is valid. If so, return `defaultVal` to preserve the caller's pre-set defaults — matching the behavior of `gopkg.in/yaml.v3`.

**Test (decode_test.go):** Added `TestDecoder_DefaultValuesWithNullNode` that reproduces the exact scenario from the issue: a nested struct key present in YAML with only comments (parsed as a null node), decoding into a pre-initialized struct with default values.
